⚡ RAT MODE ACTIVE — PLOs Only Shown
Peter Morganelli, Ph.D. · Block 5 2025

Calcium & Phosphate Metabolism

PTH, Calcitriol, and the integrated hormonal control of mineral homeostasis. Toggle RAT Mode for PLO-only prep.

Normal Serum Ca²⁺ 8.5–10.5 mg/dL
Normal Serum Pᵢ 2.5–4.5 mg/dL
PTH Blood Level 10–65 pg/mL
Key Hormone Axis PTH + Calcitriol
Ca²⁺ & Pᵢ Basics Body Balance PTH PTH Effects CaSR & Regulation Calcitriol Integration Bone Resorption FGF23 & Pᵢ Disorders
PLO 1 — RAT Objective

Describe the distribution and biological importance of calcium and phosphate

Know the forms of blood calcium, the concept of ionized vs. bound, and why albumin matters.

  • ✓
    Most abundant cation → Calcium. ~99% in bone as hydroxyapatite.
  • ✓
    Bioactive Ca²⁺ → Only free ionized (~50% of total). Bound to albumin (~40%) is NOT active.
  • ✓
    Hypoalbuminemia trap → Low total Ca²⁺ but normal ionized Ca²⁺ — NOT true hypocalcemia.
  • ✓
    Phosphate roles → ATP, second messengers, phospholipids, nucleic acids, bone mineralization.
  • ✓
    Diet → Phosphate is abundant in food; calcium is not, making dietary calcium deficiency common.
PLO 1

Mineral Physiology

Calcium & Phosphate in Health

🦴

Calcium (Ca²⁺)

  • Most abundant cation in the body
  • ~99% stored in bone as hydroxyapatite
  • Bone mineralization & teeth
  • Intracellular signaling
  • Synaptic transmission & muscle contraction
  • Cell membrane stability
  • Blood clotting
⚡

Phosphate (Pᵢ)

  • Bone & tooth mineralization
  • ATP formation (energy currency)
  • Second messengers (cAMP, IP₃)
  • Cell membrane structure (phospholipids)
  • Nucleic acid backbone
  • Rich in most foods → less frequently deficient

Forms of Blood Calcium

Total serum calcium is ~8.5–10.5 mg/dL in healthy adults, but only free ionized Ca²⁺ is biologically active and the form that is regulated by the body.

🔑 Three Fractions of Blood Calcium
Fraction% of TotalBiologically Active?Notes
Free ionized Ca²⁺~50%✅ Yes — the only regulated formSensed by parathyroids via CaSR
Bound to albumin~40%❌ NopH-sensitive — acidosis ↑ ionized Ca²⁺
Complexed to anions (esp. Pᵢ)~10%❌ No—
⚠️ Clinical Trap — Albumin & Total Calcium

Hypoalbuminemia decreases total serum Ca²⁺ but the amount of free ionized Ca²⁺ is usually normal — this is NOT true hypocalcemia. Similarly, hyperalbuminemia raises total Ca²⁺ without changing free Ca²⁺. Always check ionized calcium when albumin is abnormal!

Q: A patient has a total serum Ca²⁺ of 7.8 mg/dL. Her albumin is very low due to liver disease. Should you treat her for hypocalcemia?

Answer

Not necessarily. Low albumin causes a decrease in protein-bound calcium, reducing total serum Ca²⁺, but free ionized Ca²⁺ is likely normal. Always measure ionized Ca²⁺ directly before diagnosing and treating hypocalcemia in patients with hypoalbuminemia.

PLO 2 — RAT Objective

Describe the daily requirements and routes of calcium and phosphate handling

Know daily intake, % GI absorption, and % renal reabsorption for both Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ.

  • ✓
    Ca²⁺ daily need → ~1.0 g/day; only ~40% absorbed by small intestine.
  • ✓
    Pᵢ daily need → ~0.7 g/day; ~65% absorbed (easier to obtain from diet).
  • ✓
    Renal → ~98% of filtered Ca²⁺ AND Pᵢ normally reabsorbed; most passively in proximal tubule.
  • ✓
    Fine-tuning → PTH and calcitriol regulate the final ~2% in distal tubule (Ca²⁺) and proximal tubule (Pᵢ).
PLO 2

Mineral Physiology

Whole-Body Calcium & Phosphate Balance

Adults require approximately 1.0 g/day of dietary calcium and ~0.7 g/day of phosphate to maintain balance. Calcium absorption from the gut is tightly regulated; phosphate is more readily absorbed.

Slide 4 — Whole-body Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ balance per day (Guyton & Hall)

📊 Key Numbers to Know
ParameterCalcium (Ca²⁺)Phosphate (Pᵢ)
Daily dietary intake~1,000 mg772–2,000 mg
GI absorption~40% (calcitriol-dependent)~65% (largely passive)
Fecal excretion~800 mg/day~465–620 mg/day
Renal reabsorption~98% of filtered load~98% of filtered load
Urine excretion~200 mg/day~615–1,545 mg/day
Primary tubule siteProximal tubule (~70%)Proximal tubule (~70%)
💡 Clinical Pearl — Why Calcium Is Hard to Get

Most foods are rich in phosphate but relatively poor in calcium. This makes calcium balance harder to maintain with a poor diet, especially in postmenopausal women where calcitriol responsiveness declines.

PLO 3 — RAT Objective

Describe PTH synthesis, secretion, receptors, and PTHrP

Know the cell type, cleavage steps, half-life, receptor types, and the PTHrP connection to malignancy.

  • ✓
    Synthesizing cell → Chief cells (principal cells) of parathyroid glands.
  • ✓
    Processing → Pre-proPTH (115aa) → proPTH (90aa) → PTH (84aa); secreted as 84aa peptide.
  • ✓
    Half-life → ~3–5 minutes; degraded by kidney and liver.
  • ✓
    Receptor → PTH1R (bone + kidney), PTH2R; PTH1R is a GPCR that also binds PTHrP.
  • ✓
    PTHrP → Most common humoral cause of hypercalcemia of malignancy; mimics PTH via PTH1R.
PLO 3

Parathyroid Hormone

PTH: Synthesis, Secretion & Receptors

Slide 5 — Parathyroid anatomy and PTH biosynthesis pathway

The Parathyroid Glands

There are usually four parathyroid glands, located at the north and south poles of each lobe of the thyroid. They produce PTH, which is vital for life — removal of all parathyroids causes lethal hypocalcemia.

01

Synthesizing Cell

Chief cells (principal cells) of parathyroid glands produce PTH as a pre-prohormone.

02

Processing Steps

Pre-proPTH (115 aa) → proPTH (90 aa) → PTH (84 aa). Two cleavages, enters Golgi.

03

Secretion & Half-life

Pulsatile secretion. Blood half-life ~3–5 minutes. Degraded by kidney and liver. Normal level: 10–65 pg/mL.

04

PTH1R Receptor

G-protein coupled receptor. Found in bone and kidney. Also binds PTHrP. Most important PTH receptor.

05

PTH2R Receptor

Less common. Does not bind PTHrP. Expressed in brain, pancreas, and other tissues.

06

PTHrP

Parathyroid hormone-related peptide. Produced by placenta, fetal parathyroids, and cancers. Most common humoral cause of hypercalcemia of malignancy.

⚠️ Hypercalcemia of Malignancy — PTHrP

PTHrP binds PTH1R and mimics PTH effects. It is the most common humoral cause of hypercalcemia in cancer patients. Cancers that commonly produce PTHrP include squamous cell carcinoma of the lung, breast cancer, and renal cell carcinoma.

Q: A patient with lung cancer develops hypercalcemia. PTH levels are suppressed. What is the most likely cause?

Answer

PTHrP (Parathyroid Hormone-related Peptide) secreted by the tumor. It binds PTH1R and mimics PTH effects, raising serum Ca²⁺. Because Ca²⁺ is elevated, PTH is suppressed via negative feedback through CaSR — distinguishing this from primary hyperparathyroidism where PTH is elevated.

PLO 4 — RAT Objective

List the key effects of PTH on bone, kidney, and calcitriol production

Three main effects: bone resorption (most important), 1α-hydroxylase stimulation, renal Ca²⁺/Pᵢ handling.

  • ✓
    Effect 1 — Bone resorption → ↑ blood Ca²⁺ AND Pᵢ. Most important for maintaining blood Ca²⁺.
  • ✓
    Effect 2 — 1α-hydroxylase → Stimulates production in proximal tubule → ↑ calcitriol formation.
  • ✓
    Effect 3 — Kidney → ↑ renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption AND ↓ renal Pᵢ reabsorption (phosphaturic effect).
  • ✓
    Main PTH targets → BONE and KIDNEYS. (Calcitriol targets: bone, small intestine, parathyroid glands).
  • ✓
    Negative feedback → PTH is NOT inhibited by PTH itself — it's inhibited by ↑ Ca²⁺ PLUS ↑ calcitriol combined.
PLO 4

PTH Biology

Key Effects of PTH

Slide 6 — PTH and calcitriol working synergistically to maintain blood Ca²⁺

  1. Bone Resorption (Most Important)
    Stimulates osteoclasts → increases blood Ca²⁺ AND Pᵢ. This is the most critical acute mechanism for raising blood Ca²⁺.
  2. Stimulation of 1α-hydroxylase in Proximal Tubule
    Increases formation of calcitriol (biologically active vitamin D₃), which then stimulates Ca²⁺ absorption in the small intestine.
  3. Renal Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ Handling
    Increases Ca²⁺ reabsorption in distal tubule (via TRPV5/6 channels) and decreases Pᵢ reabsorption in proximal tubule (phosphaturic effect).
🎯 Reciprocal Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ Relationship

When PTH rises: Ca²⁺ goes up (from bone, gut, kidney) but Pᵢ goes down in blood (increased urinary excretion). The phosphate released from bone is excreted by the kidney to prevent hyperphosphatemia. This reciprocal control is a key PTH signature.

Slide 7 — After starting PTH: Ca²⁺ rises while phosphate falls (reciprocal relationship)

PTH & Calcitriol in the Kidney Tubules

Slide 8 — Distal tubule Ca²⁺ reabsorption (PTH + calcitriol) and proximal tubule Pᵢ degradation by PTH

🔬 Tubular Mechanism Detail
  • ~70% of filtered Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ is passively reabsorbed in the proximal tubule
  • In the distal tubule, PTH upregulates luminal Ca²⁺ channels TRPV5 and TRPV6 → active Ca²⁺ uptake
  • Calcitriol stimulates intracellular calbindin-D28K (Ca²⁺-binding protein) and basolateral transporters (NCX1, PMCA1b)
  • For Pᵢ: PTH stimulates lysosomal destruction of NaPi-IIa transporters in the proximal tubule → ↓ Pᵢ reabsorption → ↑ urinary Pᵢ
🔑 Critical Concept — What Inhibits PTH?

PTH production is NOT suppressed by PTH itself. Negative feedback requires the combined increase in free Ca²⁺ PLUS calcitriol acting on parathyroid chief cells. This is why vitamin D deficiency leads to chronically elevated PTH even if calcium appears adequate.

Q: Why does PTH both increase blood Ca²⁺ AND have a phosphaturic (Pᵢ-wasting) effect on the kidney? What is the physiological advantage?

Answer

PTH stimulates bone resorption, releasing both Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ into the blood. If Pᵢ reabsorption in the kidney were also increased, the resulting hyperphosphatemia would cause Ca²⁺-Pᵢ complexes to form, lowering free ionized Ca²⁺ — the opposite of the goal. By promoting Pᵢ excretion, PTH ensures that the Ca²⁺ released from bone actually raises blood Ca²⁺ rather than being sequestered in phosphate complexes.

PLO 5 — RAT Objective

Explain how the CaSR regulates PTH secretion and how cinacalcet works

Know the two states of CaSR, what second messengers are involved, and what a calcimimetic does.

  • ✓
    CaSR = "calciostat" → G-protein coupled receptor on chief cells; responds to tiny changes in free Ca²⁺.
  • ✓
    Low Ca²⁺ (relaxed state) → Gs → ↑ cAMP → ↑ PTH secretion.
  • ✓
    High Ca²⁺ (activated state) → Gq/11 → PLC → IP₃/DAG → ↓ cAMP → ↓ PTH secretion.
  • ✓
    Set point → Free Ca²⁺ ~1.1–1.2 mmol/L (4.4–4.8 mg/mL). Changes within minutes; restored in 1–2 hrs.
  • ✓
    Cinacalcet → Calcimimetic drug; activates CaSR → mimics high Ca²⁺ → ↓ PTH. Used for primary hyperparathyroidism.
PLO 5

PTH Regulation

CaSR & Regulation of PTH Production

Slide 11 — PTH secretion is exquisitely sensitive to free Ca²⁺; set point ~1.1 mmol/L (Point A)

⚙️ The Calciostat — How It Works

The Calcium-Sensing Receptor (CaSR) on parathyroid chief cells acts as a precise "calciostat." The set point is free Ca²⁺ of ~1.1–1.2 mmol/L. Slight deviations trigger changes in PTH secretion within minutes, with blood Ca²⁺ restored within 1–2 hours.

Slide 12 — CaSR molecular mechanism: relaxed state (low Ca²⁺) vs activated state (high Ca²⁺)

📉

Relaxed State — Low Ca²⁺

  • CaSR not activated
  • Gs protein active → ↑ adenylyl cyclase
  • ↑ cAMP → ↑ PKA
  • Stimulates PTH production and secretion
📈

Activated State — High Ca²⁺

  • Ca²⁺ binds and activates CaSR
  • Gq/11 → activates PLC
  • PLC → IP₃ + DAG → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺
  • Inhibits adenylyl cyclase → ↓ cAMP
  • ↓ PTH production and secretion
💊 Cinacalcet (Calcimimetic Drug)

Cinacalcet activates the CaSR, mimicking the effect of high calcium. This reduces PTH secretion and is used to treat primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) and secondary hyperparathyroidism in dialysis patients. It acts as a "molecular decoy" — telling the parathyroid gland there is plenty of calcium, even when there may not be.

Q: Through what second messenger does low blood Ca²⁺ stimulate PTH secretion?

Answer

When free Ca²⁺ falls, the CaSR enters its relaxed state, coupling to Gs protein → adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP → PKA activation → ↑ PTH secretion. When Ca²⁺ rises, CaSR activates Gq/11 → PLC → IP₃ → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ and inhibition of cAMP → ↓ PTH.

PLO 6 — RAT Objective

Describe the synthesis, regulation, and effects of calcitriol (active vitamin D₃)

Know the three organs involved in synthesis, what regulates 1α-hydroxylase and 24-hydroxylase, and the 5 major effects.

  • ✓
    Synthesis pathway → Skin (UV → cholecalciferol) → Liver (25-hydroxylase → 25-OH D₃) → Kidney (1α-hydroxylase → calcitriol).
  • ✓
    1α-hydroxylase → Stimulated by PTH; inhibited by calcitriol itself (feedback).
  • ✓
    24-hydroxylase → Stimulated by calcitriol and Ca²⁺; inhibited by PTH. Produces inactive 24,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol.
  • ✓
    Most important effect → Stimulates GI absorption of dietary Ca²⁺ (and Pᵢ) by SI enterocytes.
  • ✓
    Serum marker → 25-hydroxycholecalciferol (stable); used to assess vitamin D status (NOT calcitriol which has a short half-life).
PLO 6

Calcitriol — Active Vitamin D₃

Calcitriol: Synthesis, Regulation & Effects

⚠️ Vitamin D Deficiency Is Common

Approximately ~25% of US adults are vitamin D deficient and ~50% are borderline deficient. Deficiency is linked to hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, osteoporosis, and has been associated with autoimmune diseases including lupus, MS, rheumatoid arthritis, and autoimmune thyroiditis.

Synthesis Pathway

Slide 14 — Vitamin D₃ synthesis: skin → liver → kidney; 1α-hydroxylase and 24-hydroxylase regulation

  1. Skin — UV Light → Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D₃)
    7-dehydrocholesterol (provitamin D) in keratinocytes is converted to cholecalciferol by UV light exposure. Also obtained from dietary vitamin D₃.
  2. Liver — 25-hydroxylase → 25-hydroxycholecalciferol
    Cholecalciferol undergoes 25-hydroxylation in the liver. This product is stable in plasma and is the standard marker of vitamin D status clinically.
  3. Kidney — 1α-hydroxylase → Calcitriol (Active!)
    25-OH-D₃ is converted to 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol (calcitriol) in the proximal tubule. This step is rate-limiting and tightly regulated.
🔑 Regulation of the Two Kidney Hydroxylases
EnzymeStimulated ByInhibited ByProduct
1α-hydroxylasePTH ↑, Low Ca²⁺/PᵢCalcitriol (feedback), FGF23Calcitriol (ACTIVE)
24-hydroxylaseCalcitriol, High Ca²⁺PTH24,25-dihydroxy D₃ (INACTIVE)

Major Effects of Calcitriol

Slide 15 — Calcitriol regulates Ca²⁺ absorption by SI enterocytes via VDR and calbindin-D

🍽️

GI Absorption (Most Important)

Stimulates Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ absorption by SI enterocytes. Upregulates TRPV5/6 channels, calbindin-D, and basolateral transporters (NCX1, PMCA1b).

🔄

PTH Feedback Inhibition

Acts on parathyroid chief cells to inhibit PTH production — contributes to negative feedback loop together with elevated Ca²⁺.

🦴

Bone Resorption

Synergizes with PTH to stimulate osteoblasts → RANKL → osteoclast activation → Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ release from hydroxyapatite.

🫘

Renal Reabsorption

Enhances Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ reabsorption in the distal tubule by upregulating transporters and intracellular binding proteins.

🛡️

Immune Modulation

Deficiency linked to autoimmune conditions: lupus, MS, rheumatoid arthritis, autoimmune thyroiditis. Acts via vitamin D receptor (VDR) in immune cells.

Q: A patient has a low serum level of 25-hydroxycholecalciferol but a normal serum calcitriol. What does this tell you, and which measurement is used to clinically assess vitamin D status?

Answer

25-hydroxycholecalciferol is the clinical marker for vitamin D status — it is stable in plasma and reflects overall vitamin D stores. Calcitriol has a short half-life and its levels can be normal or even elevated despite deficiency (due to compensatory PTH driving 1α-hydroxylase). This patient has inadequate vitamin D stores (true vitamin D deficiency) despite a misleadingly normal active form measurement.

PLO 7 — RAT Objective

Trace the integrated hormonal response to an acute hypocalcemic challenge

Follow the chain: low Ca²⁺ → ↑PTH → calcitriol + kidney + bone → normocalcemia. Know why Pᵢ stays normal.

  • ✓
    Trigger → Serum Ca²⁺ <8.5 mg/dL → CaSR detects → ↑ PTH within minutes.
  • ✓
    Step 1 (PTH) → ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → ↑ calcitriol; ↑ renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption; ↓ renal Pᵢ reabsorption.
  • ✓
    Step 2 (Calcitriol) → ↑ GI absorption of Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ.
  • ✓
    Step 3 (Bone) → PTH + calcitriol → RANKL → osteoclasts → ↑ serum Ca²⁺ AND Pᵢ from bone.
  • ✓
    Why no hyperphosphatemia? → Increased intestinal Pᵢ is offset by PTH-driven ↑ renal Pᵢ excretion → normophosphatemia maintained.
PLO 7

Integrated Response

Response to Acute Hypocalcemia

Slide 16 — Complete integrated response to low serum Ca²⁺: PTH → calcitriol → bone, gut, kidney

  1. Low Ca²⁺ → ↑ PTH Secretion
    Parathyroids detect ↓ free Ca²⁺ via CaSR → PTH released within minutes.
  2. PTH → ↑ Calcitriol + ↑ Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption
    PTH stimulates renal 1α-hydroxylase → ↑ calcitriol; and directly increases TRPV5/6 Ca²⁺ channels in distal tubule → more Ca²⁺ saved.
  3. Calcitriol → ↑ GI Absorption of Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ
    Calcitriol acts on SI enterocytes to upregulate Ca²⁺ channels, calbindin-D, and transporters.
  4. PTH + Calcitriol → ↑ Bone Resorption
    Synergistic stimulation of osteoblasts → RANKL expression → osteoclast maturation → dissolution of hydroxyapatite → ↑ serum Ca²⁺ and Pᵢ.
  5. Maintain Normophosphatemia
    Extra Pᵢ released from bone and absorbed from gut is excreted by the kidney (PTH inhibits renal Pᵢ reabsorption) → serum Pᵢ stays in normal range.
🔑 Main Targets Summary
HormonePrimary Target OrgansKey Outcome
PTHBone, Kidney↑ Ca²⁺ (acute), ↑ calcitriol, ↓ urinary Pᵢ
CalcitriolSmall Intestine, Bone, Parathyroid↑ GI Ca²⁺ absorption (most important), feedback ↓ PTH
CalcitoninBone (osteoclasts)↓ bone resorption if hypercalcemic; minor role in humans
PLO 7b — RAT Objective

Explain the RANKL/RANK/OPG mechanism by which PTH and calcitriol stimulate bone resorption

Know the two major points: RANKL production by osteoblasts and OPG inhibition.

  • ✓
    Major Point 1 → PTH + calcitriol stimulate osteoblasts to produce RANKL → RANKL binds RANK on preosteoclasts → differentiate into mature osteoclasts.
  • ✓
    Major Point 2 → PTH + calcitriol INHIBIT osteoprotegerin (OPG), the decoy receptor for RANKL → less OPG means more free RANKL → more osteoclasts → more resorption.
  • ✓
    Calcitonin → Secreted by thyroid C cells; inhibits osteoclast activity; minor role in humans; calcitonin mimetics used for hypercalcemia.
PLO 7b

Bone Physiology

RANKL/RANK/OPG — Bone Resorption Mechanism

Slide 17 — RANKL/RANK/OPG axis: how PTH and calcitriol drive osteoclast-mediated bone resorption

🔓

Major Point 1 — RANKL Drives Resorption

  • PTH + calcitriol act on osteoblasts
  • ↑ RANKL (receptor activator of NF-κB ligand)
  • RANKL binds RANK on preosteoclasts
  • Preosteoclasts → mature osteoclasts
  • Osteoclasts dissolve hydroxyapatite → liberates Ca²⁺ & Pᵢ
🛡️

Major Point 2 — OPG Inhibition

  • PTH + calcitriol inhibit OPG production
  • OPG is the decoy receptor for RANKL
  • Less OPG → more free RANKL available
  • → More osteoclast formation
  • → More bone resorption → more Ca²⁺ released
💊 Clinical Connection — Denosumab

Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody against RANKL. By blocking RANKL, it prevents osteoclast differentiation and reduces bone resorption. It is used to treat osteoporosis and bone metastases — essentially mimicking the effect of high OPG levels.

🔬 Calcitonin — The Minor Counter-Regulator

Calcitonin is a calcium-lowering peptide secreted by thyroid parafollicular C cells. It inhibits osteoclast activity when blood Ca²⁺ is elevated. Its role is relatively minor in adult humans, but calcitonin mimetics are used clinically to treat hypercalcemia in certain conditions (e.g., Paget's disease, hypercalcemia of malignancy).

Q: PTH acts on osteoblasts, not directly on osteoclasts. How does it still cause bone resorption?

Answer

PTH receptors (PTH1R) are expressed on osteoblasts. PTH stimulates osteoblasts to produce RANKL and inhibit OPG. RANKL then binds RANK on preosteoclasts, driving their differentiation into active osteoclasts. The osteoblast thus acts as the intermediary — it "passes the signal" to drive osteoclast-mediated resorption. This explains why the cell that makes bone is also involved in bone destruction under PTH stimulation.

PLO 8a — RAT Objective

Describe how FGF23 regulates phosphate balance, particularly in hyperphosphatemia and CKD

Know what triggers FGF23, its two main effects, and why CKD leads to secondary hyperparathyroidism.

  • ✓
    FGF23 source → Secreted by osteocytes in response to hyperphosphatemia (and high calcitriol).
  • ✓
    Effect 1 → Inhibits renal Pᵢ reabsorption (destroys NaPi transporters) → ↑ urinary Pᵢ excretion.
  • ✓
    Effect 2 → Inhibits 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol → less negative feedback on PTH → ↑ PTH (SHPT).
  • ✓
    CKD cascade → ↓ GFR → ↑ Pᵢ → ↑ FGF23 → ↓ calcitriol → ↑ PTH → bone resorption → attempted hypercalcemia BUT ↓ GI absorption prevents it.
PLO 8a

Phosphate Regulation

FGF23 & Phosphate Homeostasis

Slide 18 — FGF23 regulation of phosphate balance and its role in secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD

🔬 FGF23 — The Phosphate Hormone

Fibroblast Growth Factor 23 (FGF23) is produced by osteocytes in response to elevated blood phosphate (and elevated calcitriol). It acts primarily on the kidney to reduce phosphate load.

🦴

Source

Osteocytes secrete FGF23 in response to hyperphosphatemia and high calcitriol.

🫘

Renal Effect 1

Inhibits renal Pᵢ reabsorption (destroys NaPi-IIa transporters) → ↑ urinary Pᵢ excretion.

🚫

Renal Effect 2

Inhibits 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol formation → reduces GI Ca²⁺/Pᵢ absorption.

📈

PTH Consequence

↓ Calcitriol = less negative feedback on parathyroid → ↑ PTH secretion → increased bone resorption.

Slide 19 — Summary of hyperphosphatemia effects in secondary hyperparathyroidism (SHPT in CKD)

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) — SHPT Cascade

⚠️ CKD → Secondary Hyperparathyroidism
  1. ↓ GFR → ↓ renal Pᵢ excretion → hyperphosphatemia
  2. ↑ Pᵢ stimulates osteocytes → ↑ FGF23 → inhibits 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol
  3. ↓ Calcitriol → ↓ GI Ca²⁺ absorption → tendency to hypocalcemia
  4. ↓ Calcitriol → less feedback inhibition of PTH → ↑ PTH (SHPT)
  5. Despite ↑ PTH trying to raise Ca²⁺ via bone resorption, Ca²⁺ may be low to normal because ↓ GI absorption + Ca²⁺-Pᵢ complexes deposit in tissues
💊 Management of CKD-MBD

Treatment targets include: phosphate binders (reduce GI Pᵢ absorption), active vitamin D analogues (replace calcitriol), and cinacalcet (calcimimetic to reduce PTH). Parathyroidectomy in severe refractory SHPT.

Q: A dialysis patient with CKD has high PTH, low calcitriol, and low-normal calcium with hyperphosphatemia. Explain the chain of events that led to this.

Answer

1. CKD → ↓ GFR → ↓ phosphate excretion → hyperphosphatemia. 2. Hyperphosphatemia → osteocytes release FGF23 → inhibits renal 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol. 3. ↓ Calcitriol → ↓ GI Ca²⁺ absorption + less negative feedback on parathyroids → ↑ PTH (SHPT). 4. Despite high PTH, Ca²⁺ is low-to-normal because high Pᵢ binds Ca²⁺, calcitriol cannot drive GI absorption, and Ca²⁺-phosphate deposits in soft tissues.

PLO 8b — RAT Objective

Differentiate causes and features of hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia

Know PHPT vs SHPT, the role of PTHrP, and clinical symptoms of both hyper- and hypocalcemia.

  • ✓
    Hypercalcemia = total serum Ca²⁺ >10.5 mg/dL. PHPT most common cause (benign adenoma, elevated PTH).
  • ✓
    PHPT symptoms → "Stones, bones, groans, moans" — kidney stones, bone loss, GI issues, neuropsychiatric, fatigue, HTN.
  • ✓
    SHPT → ↑ PTH driven by external factors (↑ Pᵢ, ↓ vitamin D, hypocalcemia). Total Ca²⁺ may be low to elevated.
  • ✓
    Hypocalcemia = total serum Ca²⁺ <8.5 mg/dL. Most common cause: vitamin D deficiency. Also hypoparathyroidism (post-thyroidectomy) or glucocorticoid therapy.
  • ✓
    Hypocalcemia symptoms → Tetany, muscle spasms, seizures, paresthesia, arrhythmias, heart failure.
PLO 8b

Clinical Disorders

Disorders of Calcium Balance

Hypercalcemia (Total Serum Ca²⁺ >10.5 mg/dL)

🔴

Primary Hyperparathyroidism (PHPT)

  • Most common cause of hypercalcemia
  • Usually a benign adenoma in one parathyroid
  • Elevated serum PTH is diagnostic
  • May be associated with vitamin D deficiency
  • Asymptomatic (>80%) — found incidentally
  • Mostly postmenopausal females
🟣

Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (SHPT)

  • ↑ PTH driven by external stimuli (not primary parathyroid disease)
  • Common causes: ↑ Pᵢ (CKD), vitamin D deficiency, hypocalcemia
  • Total serum Ca²⁺ may be low, normal, or elevated
  • Treat the underlying cause + supplement Ca²⁺/vitamin D
⚠️ Symptomatic PHPT — "Stones, Bones, Groans, Moans"
  • Stones → Kidney stones (hypercalciuria), organ calcification (heart, kidneys, vessels)
  • Bones → Bone loss (osteoclast overactivity), bone pain, fractures
  • Groans → GI issues (nausea, constipation, peptic ulcers)
  • Moans → Cognitive/neurological dysfunction, depression, skeletal muscle weakness, fatigue
  • Plus → Hypertension

Hypocalcemia (Total Serum Ca²⁺ <8.5 mg/dL)

📋 Causes of Hypocalcemia
CauseMechanismPTH Level
Vitamin D deficiency / poor dietMost common; ↓ calcitriol → ↓ GI Ca²⁺ absorption↑ (SHPT)
Glucocorticoid therapyInterferes with vitamin D action and Ca²⁺ absorption↑
HypoparathyroidismDamage to parathyroids (post-thyroidectomy, autoimmune)↓ or absent
PseudohypoparathyroidismTarget organ resistance to PTH (PTH is normal/elevated)↑ (non-responsive)
CKD / SHPT↓ 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol + ↑ Pᵢ complexes↑↑
⚠️ Symptoms of Hypocalcemia — Neuromuscular Excitability
  • Tetany — involuntary muscle contractions
  • Muscle spasms and cramps
  • Convulsions and seizures
  • Paresthesia (tingling, numbness)
  • Cardiac arrhythmias
  • Heart failure (HFrEF)

Why? Low Ca²⁺ increases membrane permeability and neuronal excitability — cells fire spontaneously.

Q: How do you distinguish Primary Hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) from the hypercalcemia of malignancy (PTHrP) using lab values?

Answer

In PHPT: serum PTH is elevated (adenoma producing excess PTH) and serum PTHrP is normal. In hypercalcemia of malignancy: serum PTH is suppressed (high Ca²⁺ from PTHrP feeds back to inhibit PTH) while serum PTHrP is elevated. Key: PTHrP is immunologically distinct from PTH in most assays, so a PTHrP assay can confirm the diagnosis. Both cause hypercalcemia, hypophosphatemia (Pᵢ-wasting), and elevated calcitriol (PTH drives 1α-hydroxylase).
